data_in.description: Raw LC-MS data in mzML format from a public metabolomics repository (MetaboLights or MassIVE accession)

data_out.description: Feature table with rows as detected metabolite features and columns as samples, including peak intensity values
Summary statistics report containing feature count, sample count, and peak intensity descriptive statistics (mean, median, SD, min, max)

domain_knowledge: MetaboAnalystR 4.0 implements a unified workflow that handles raw LC-MS data input through feature detection to quantification in a single pipeline.
Feature tables in metabolomics have samples as columns and detected metabolite features (peaks) as rows, with intensity values representing peak area or height.
Summary statistics on peak intensities reveal data quality, dynamic range, and the prevalence of weak versus strong signals across the dataset.
Public LC-MS datasets deposited in MetaboLights or MassIVE follow standardized formats (mzML, netCDF) to enable reproducible reanalysis.

direct_checks: verify that MetaboAnalystR package can be loaded and executed against a public LC-MS dataset (e.g., from MetaboLights or MassIVE accession)
file_exists: check that the output feature table artifact is generated (expected format: CSV, TSV, or R data frame)
row_count_equals or row_count_in_range: verify the feature table contains at least one feature (row > 0) and at least one sample (column > 0)
field_present: confirm the output table contains columns for feature identifiers, sample identifiers, and intensity or abundance values
value_in_range: check that peak intensity summary statistics (mean, median, min, max, standard deviation) are non-negative and finite
script_runs: verify that the end-to-end LC-MS workflow script executes without fatal errors on the selected public dataset
expert_review: assess whether the feature table dimensions and intensity distributions are reasonable for the chosen LC-MS dataset (e.g., consistent with dataset documentation or comparable published analyses)
evaluate whether peak intensity summary statistics and feature recovery align with expected metabolomic signal characteristics (no anomalously sparse or saturated distributions)

cmd: library(MetaboAnalystR); mSet <- InitDataObjects('pihc', 'stat', FALSE); mSet <- Read.MSdata(mSet, '{inputs.raw_lcms_data}'); mSet <- SanityCheckData(mSet); mSet <- ReplaceMin(mSet); mSet <- FilterVariable(mSet, 'iqr', 25); mSet <- Normalization(mSet, 'ColNorm', 'NULL', 'NULL', TRUE); ft <- mSet$dataSet$norm; write.csv(ft, 'feature_table.csv'); stats <- data.frame(feature_count = nrow(ft), sample_count = ncol(ft), intensity_mean = mean(as.matrix(ft), na.rm = TRUE), intensity_median = median(as.matrix(ft), na.rm = TRUE), intensity_sd = sd(as.vector(as.matrix(ft)), na.rm = TRUE), intensity_min = min(as.matrix(ft), na.rm = TRUE), intensity_max = max(as.matrix(ft), na.rm = TRUE)); write.csv(stats, 'summary_statistics.csv', row.names = FALSE); cat('Analysis complete: ', nrow(ft), ' features x ', ncol(ft), ' samples\n')

Synthesis grounding: the following tools/outputs were NOT found in the source paper and are inferred — verify before use: Summary statistics report containing feature count, sample count, and peak intensity descriptive statistics (mean, median, SD, min, max).
workflow_description: 1. Load raw LC-MS data (mzML or equivalent format) from a public repository (MetaboLights or MassIVE) into MetaboAnalystR 4.0. 2. Execute the unified LC-MS workflow to generate a feature detection and quantification table. 3. Extract feature table dimensions (number of rows as features, number of columns as samples) and compute summary statistics on peak intensity values (mean, median, standard deviation, min, max). 4. Compile results into a structured output report.
